restore-without-rewriting-history.label: Restore without Rewriting History
restore-without-rewriting-history.summary: Undo working-tree or staging mistakes safely while leaving existing commits untouched.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Discard One Unstaged Mistake
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`notes.txt` has an unwanted working-tree edit. Restore it from HEAD, then verify that the repository is clean.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: git restore PATH replaces an unstaged working-tree version with the version recorded in the index/HEAD.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`notes.txt` has an unwanted working-tree edit. Restore it from HEAD, then verify that the repository is clean.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Approved release note.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Accidental replacement.
' > notes.txt
cd ..

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Approved release note.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Accidental replacement.
' > notes.txt
cd ..

0.message: Do not rewrite shared history in this Git practice task.
Run `git restore notes.txt`, then check your answer again.

1.message: Run `git status --short`, then check your answer again.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Unstage Without Losing the Edit
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`draft.txt` is staged, but it should not be in the next commit. Unstage it while keeping its working-tree content, then read short status.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: git restore --staged moves the selected path out of the index while preserving the working-tree edit.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`draft.txt` is staged, but it should not be in the next commit. Unstage it while keeping its working-tree content, then read short status.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Draft v1
' > draft.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Draft v2
' > draft.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -- draft.txt

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Draft v1
' > draft.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Draft v2
' > draft.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -- draft.txt

0.message: Do not rewrite shared history in this Git practice task.
Run `git restore --staged draft.txt`, then check your answer again.

1.message: Run `git status --short`, then check your answer again.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Unstage, Then Discard
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`summary.txt` is staged with an accidental rewrite. First unstage it, then restore the working file from HEAD and confirm a clean state.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: Staged and working-tree state are separate boundaries, so a staged mistake may require two deliberate restore commands.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.solutionCode:
git restore --staged summary.txt
git restore summary.txt
git status --short
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`summary.txt` is staged with an accidental rewrite. First unstage it, then restore the working file from HEAD and confirm a clean state.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Verify History Was Not Rewritten
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

A mistaken edit has already been restored. Read the latest commit and clean status to confirm that correction changed the working tree, not existing history.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: Safe restore operations adjust the index or working tree; they do not alter the commit already stored in history.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

A mistaken edit has already been restored. Read the latest commit and clean status to confirm that correction changed the working tree, not existing history.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
printf %s 'mode=production
' > config.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add release configuration'

#!/usr/bin/env bash

git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
printf %s 'mode=production
' > config.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add release configuration'

0.message: Do not rewrite shared history in this Git practice task.
Run `git log -1 --oneline`, then check your answer again.

1.message: Run `git status --short`, then check your answer again.

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of git restore
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git restore` command?
single-choice-1.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: git restore changes the working tree, while git restore --staged changes the index; neither rewrites existing commits.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

options.a: To revert changes in the working directory
options.b: To commit changes to the repository

single-choice-2.title: Effect of git restore --staged
single-choice-2.prompt: What happens when you use `git restore --staged` on a file?
single-choice-2.hint: Consider what `git restore --staged` does to the staging area.

help.concept: The `git restore --staged` command is used to unstage files while keeping their changes.
help.hint_1: Think about how `git restore --staged` affects the staging area.
help.hint_2: `git restore --staged` removes the file from the staging area but keeps its changes.

options.a: The file is removed from the staging area but changes are kept
options.b: The file is deleted from the repository
options.c: The file is committed to the repository
options.d: The file is reverted to the last committed state

multi-choice-1.title: Choosing the Correct Restore Command
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which commands can be used to revert changes in the working directory and unstage a file?
multi-choice-1.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: git restore changes the working tree, while git restore --staged changes the index; neither rewrites existing commits.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of Operations for Restoring Files
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps to revert changes in the working directory and unstage a file.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

tokens.t1: Revert changes in the working directory

fill-blank-1.title: Command to Unstage a File
fill-blank-1.prompt: Fill in the blank to unstage a file while keeping its changes: `git restore --[blank1] notes.txt`
fill-blank-1.hint: The option to unstage a file is `--the missing term`.

help.concept: The `git restore --the missing term` command is used to unstage files while keeping their changes.
help.hint_1: The option to unstage a file is `--the missing term`.
help.hint_2: Use `--the missing term` to unstage a file.

fill-blank-2.title: Command to Revert Changes
fill-blank-2.prompt: Fill in the blank to revert changes in the working directory: `git [blank1] notes.txt`
fill-blank-2.hint: The command to revert changes is `the missing term`.

help.concept: The `git the missing term` command is used to revert changes in the working directory.
help.hint_1: The command to revert changes is `the missing term`.
help.hint_2: Use `the missing term` to revert changes.

restore-sketch-0.title: Restore an Unstaged File
restore-sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
To discard an unstaged edit and return a tracked file to the staged or committed version, run:

```bash
git restore checklist.md
```

This changes the working file. The discarded text is not preserved by a new commit, so inspect the diff first.

Use restore only when you are certain the working-tree edit is unwanted.

restore-sketch-1.title: Unstage Without Discarding
restore-sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
To remove a file from the staging area while keeping its current contents, run:

```bash
git restore --staged checklist.md
```

The change moves back to the unstaged section of status. Your work remains in the file.

This is the safe choice when the edit is valid but does not belong in the next commit.

restore-sketch-2.title: Choose the Layer You Mean
restore-sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
Plain `git restore path` affects the working tree. `git restore --staged path` affects the staging area. The extra option changes which layer Git updates.

Read `git status --short` before choosing. A left-column mark points to staged state; a right-column mark points to working-tree state.

The goal is precise correction, not history rewriting.

restore-sketch-3.title: Verify Before Moving On
restore-sketch-3.bodyMarkdown:
After restoring or unstaging, run status and the appropriate diff again.

A successful working-tree restore removes the unstaged diff. A successful unstage moves the change from `git diff --staged` back to plain `git diff`.

Verification makes the undo visible and protects the work you meant to keep.
